generated_oml:
// C3: Physical Sensing Components
instance TemperatureSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Heterogeneous sensor capturing operational metrics including temperature readings for contextual data aggregation as defined in Digital Shadow Reference Model"
    DTDFVocab:producedData TemperatureDataTrace
]

instance VibrationSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor measuring vibration parameters for performance monitoring and anomaly detection in manufacturing systems"
    DTDFVocab:producedData VibrationDataTrace
]

instance OperationalMetricsSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor collecting general operational metrics (e.g., throughput, efficiency) for system-wide contextual analysis"
    DTDFVocab:producedData OperationalMetricsTrace
]

instance EnvironmentalParameterSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Sensor measuring environmental parameters (e.g., humidity, pressure) relevant to system performance"
    DTDFVocab:producedData EnvironmentalParameterTrace
]

// C4: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction
instance TemperatureDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TemperatureSensor
]

instance VibrationDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom VibrationSensor
]

instance OperationalMetricsTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom OperationalMetricsSensor
]

instance EnvironmentalParameterTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom EnvironmentalParameterSensor
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance ContextualDataAggregationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Model implementing aggregation and abstraction of raw sensor data into meaningful contextual traces for DT purposes as per Digital Shadow Reference Model"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationFramework
]

instance BehavioralStateMachineModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "SysML v1.7 state machine model representing system behavior for real-time analytics and control logic implementation"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationEngine
]

instance PhysicsBasedSimulationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Simulink-based physics model for dynamic system analysis and closed-loop optimization"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationEngine
]

instance DigitalShadowTraceData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated contextual data traces from physical sensors processed through digital shadow systems for DT interpretation"
    DTDFVocab:fromData TemperatureDataTrace, VibrationDataTrace, OperationalMetricsTrace, EnvironmentalParameterTrace
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DataAggregationFramework
]

instance ProcessedOperationalMetrics : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Post-processed operational metrics including derived KPIs and performance indicators for system monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:fromData OperationalMetricsTrace
    DTDFVocab:inputTo OptimizationAlgorithm
]

// C11: Enablers
instance DataAggregationFramework : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Software framework implementing digital shadow reference model for automated one-way data flow from physical system to DT representations"
    DTDFVocab:enables ContextualAnalysisService, AnomalyDetectionService
]

instance SimulationEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink environment for dynamic system simulation and real-time data processing integration"
    DTDFVocab:enables SystemBehaviorSimulationService
]

instance OptimizationAlgorithm : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Embedded optimization engine for parameter tuning and resource allocation based on simulated or historical data"
    DTDFVocab:enables ParameterTuningService, ResourceAllocationService
]

instance StateMachineRuntime : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MontiArc Automaton runtime environment executing state-based behavior models for system reconfiguration"
    DTDFVocab:enables SystemReconfigurationService
]

instance SysMLModelingTool : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML toolchain for functional/logical architecture modeling and behavioral state machine implementation"
    DTDFVocab:enables ArchitectureModelingService
]

// C6: Services
instance ContextualAnalysisService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service performing contextual analysis of aggregated data traces for performance monitoring and trend analysis"
    DTDFVocab:provides PerformanceTrendInsight, AnomalyDetectionInsight
]

instance AnomalyDetectionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service identifying deviations from expected behavior patterns using statistical methods on processed data traces"
    DTDFVocab:provides AnomalyAlertAction
]

instance SystemBehaviorSimulationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service executing physics-based simulations for system behavior prediction and failure forecasting"
    DTDFVocab:provides FailurePredictionInsight
]

instance ParameterTuningService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service optimizing system parameters based on simulation results and historical performance data"
    DTDFVocab:provides OptimizationRecommendationInsight
]

instance ResourceAllocationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service managing computational and energy resource distribution based on system load predictions"
    DTDFVocab:provides ResourceAllocationInsight
]

instance SystemReconfigurationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service enabling operational mode switching and system reconfiguration via state machine execution"
    DTDFVocab:provides ReconfigurationAction
]

instance ArchitectureModelingService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Service supporting MBSE workflows for requirements-to-physical architecture derivation using SysML"
    DTDFVocab:provides ArchitectureDesignInsight
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance PerformanceTrendInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Long-term performance trend analysis derived from contextual data aggregation for strategic decision making"
]

instance AnomalyDetectionInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Identified anomalies in system behavior patterns requiring immediate investigation"
]

instance FailurePredictionInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Predicted system failures based on simulation models and degradation pattern analysis"
]

instance OptimizationRecommendationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Parameter tuning recommendations generated through optimization algorithms for improved system performance"
]

instance ResourceAllocationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Resource distribution suggestions based on predictive load analysis for efficient system operation"
]

instance ArchitectureDesignInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Validated architecture designs derived from requirements through functional/logical layers using MBSE methodologies"
]

instance AnomalyAlertAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated alert generation for detected anomalies in system operation"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance ReconfigurationAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "System operational mode switching triggered by state machine execution based on DT insights"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

// C1: Environment
instance ManufacturingSystemEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains TemperatureSensor, VibrationSensor, OperationalMetricsSensor, EnvironmentalParameterSensor,
                 DataAggregationFramework, SimulationEngine, OptimizationAlgorithm,
                 StateMachineRuntime, SysMLModelingTool
]

instance DigitalTwinEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains ContextualDataAggregationModel, BehavioralStateMachineModel, PhysicsBasedSimulationModel,
                 DigitalShadowTraceData, ProcessedOperationalMetrics,
                 ContextualAnalysisService, AnomalyDetectionService, SystemBehaviorSimulationService,
                 ParameterTuningService, ResourceAllocationService, SystemReconfigurationService,
                 ArchitectureModelingService
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system’s temporal aspects are governed by the need for synchronized data flows between physical and digital components, though explicit synchronization frequencies or time scales are not detailed in the provided documents. However, the context implies that **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies—such as **SysML** (System Modeling Language) and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **AADL** or **MontiArc**—are used to model system behavior at varying levels of abstraction (e.g., functional, logical, physical). These models are often integrated with **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) or **general-purpose languages** (e.g., C++, Java) to enforce temporal granularity and latency requirements for services. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Kritzinger et al.) suggests that data aggregation and abstraction for Digital Shadows (a precursor to Digital Twins) occur in real-time or near-real-time for specific purposes, implying that Digital Twin systems would similarly require **low-latency data processing** to maintain consistency between physical and digital representations. No explicit time scales (e.g., millisecond, second-level granularity) or synchronization protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT) are mentioned, but the integration of heterogeneous tools and stakeholders necessitates **adaptive temporal coordination mechanisms** to handle varying service time scales."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system’s orchestration and coordination are framed within **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, which rely on **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME**, **AADL**, or **MontiArc** to define systems from a **component & connector perspective**. These ADLs describe **systems’ components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition**, enabling modular and reusable Digital Twin components. The integration of **behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automaton) with simulation tools (e.g., **Simulink**) or general-purpose code (e.g., C++, Java) suggests a **service-oriented architecture** where Digital Twin components interact via well-defined interfaces. The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** highlights the need for **active components** (e.g., software systems) to process incoming data, implying that Digital Twin orchestration involves **resource management** (e.g., data aggregation, abstraction) and **system-wide coordination** to ensure consistency. No specific **architecture patterns** (e.g., event-driven, peer-to-peer) or **resource allocation strategies** (e.g., load balancing) are mentioned, but the context implies a **heterogeneous, tool-agnostic** approach to system-wide coordination."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing multiple abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Transition management between these stages is facilitated via **methodologies such as RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM (Object-Oriented Systems Engineering Method)**, **SPES (Systematic Process for Engineering Systems)**, and **SMArDT (Smart Digital Twin Methodology)**. Each layer employs distinct modeling notations: **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for functional/logical architecture (e.g., SysML IBDs for component-connector perspectives), **ADLs (Architecture Description Languages)** like **AADL (Avionics System Architecture Language)** or **ACME (Architecture Description Language)** for structural modeling, and **state machines/automata** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton) for behavioral representation. Governance is implicitly addressed through **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** (per [31]) and **toolchain interoperability**, where models are exchanged across **SysML, Simulink, general-purpose languages (C++, Java), and simulation tools**. The lifecycle transitions are governed by **abstraction refinement**—mapping high-level requirements to low-level physical implementations—with **version control** implicitly required for iterative refinement across tools.

The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (per [32]) defines a passive data aggregation phase for contextual traces, which aligns with early lifecycle stages (e.g., data collection and abstraction) before evolving into an active DT with **bidirectional data flow** and **interpretive components** (per [35]–[37])."
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system addresses **fidelity and validity** through:
- **Model fidelity requirements**: Achieved via **multi-layered MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT [18–22]) to ensure consistency across requirements, functional, logical, and physical layers. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** like AADL or SysML IBDs enforce structural fidelity by defining component interfaces, connectors, and compositions. Behavioral fidelity is ensured via **simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink) integrated with ADLs for dynamic validation,
- **Validation and verification methods**: Implicitly supported by **MBSE practices** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automaton) and **simulation-based testing** (via tools like Simulink or general-purpose languages). The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** [32] suggests **data trace aggregation** as a validation mechanism for contextual accuracy,
- **Uncertainty sources and quantification**: Not explicitly detailed, but inferred from **sensor data aggregation** (digital shadows) and **model abstraction** across tools/languages. Uncertainty may arise from **data trace abstraction** or **tool interoperability gaps**,
- **Error detection and correction mechanisms**: Not described, though **active software components** in DTs (per [35–37]) could theoretically include error correction via real-time data processing or model reconciliation,
- **Quality assurance processes**: Implicitly tied to **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP) and **ADL-based consistency checks** (e.g., AADL/Acme for structural validity). The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** [32] highlights **purpose-driven aggregation** as a quality control step for contextual data traces.
"
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
The **technical network infrastructure** for Digital Twin (DT) systems is designed to support **low-latency, high-reliability communication** between digital and physical components, with the following technical specifications:

1. **Communication Protocols**: The document implicitly supports **OPC UA** (as mentioned in physical-to-virtual interaction) for bidirectional communication, though explicit mention of protocols for virtual-to-physical control is absent. Other protocols like **MQTT** (for lightweight messaging) or **TCP/IP** (for general network communication) are likely candidates, given their prevalence in industrial DT architectures.

2. **Network Topology and Architecture**: The infrastructure follows a **distributed or hybrid architecture**, integrating **cloud, edge, and on-premise** components to accommodate latency-sensitive operations. The document does not specify exact topologies (e.g., mesh, star, or hierarchical), but the reference to **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** suggests a **modular, scalable** design to accommodate diverse communication needs.

3. **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements**: While not quantified, the document implies **real-time or near-real-time** requirements for DT operations, necessitating **low-latency networks** (e.g., <100ms for critical control loops). Bandwidth demands depend on data volume (e.g., high-resolution sensor streams) and are likely managed via **QoS (Quality of Service) policies** in the network.

4. **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms**: To ensure uninterrupted operation, the infrastructure may employ **failover mechanisms**, **load balancing**, or **dual-network paths** (e.g., redundant Ethernet links). The document does not detail specific redundancy strategies but aligns with industrial standards for **high-availability networks**.

5. **Security Protocols**: Security is implied through the mention of **OPC UA’s** built-in encryption and authentication features. Additional protocols like **TLS/SSL** for data-in-transit security or **IPsec** for network-level protection are likely integrated to prevent tampering or eavesdropping. The document does not specify exact security frameworks but emphasizes the need for **secure communication** in DT systems.
"
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
The **deployment infrastructure** for Digital Twin (DT) systems is characterized by **flexible, scalable hosting models** to accommodate computational and storage demands, with the following technical specifics:

1. **Hosting Platforms**: The infrastructure supports **cloud-based**, **edge computing**, and **on-premise** deployment models. Cloud platforms (e.g., AWS, Azure) provide **scalable resources** for large-scale DTs, while edge deployment reduces latency for localized operations. On-premise solutions are used for **high-security or legacy system integration**.

2. **Computational Requirements**: The document does not specify exact CPU/GPU needs, but DT systems typically require **high-performance computing** for real-time simulations (e.g., using **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation tools). The computational load depends on the DT’s complexity (e.g., physics-based modeling, AI/ML inference).

3. **Storage Requirements**: Storage is allocated for **historical data traces**, **model artifacts**, and **metadata**. The document references **data aggregation and abstraction**, implying storage needs for time-series data (e.g., petabytes for industrial DTs) and **model repositories** (e.g., SysML diagrams, ADL specifications).

4. **Scalability Mechanisms**: The infrastructure must support **horizontal scaling** (e.g., adding nodes to a cloud cluster) and **vertical scaling** (e.g., upgrading server resources). The document highlights the use of **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., SysML, ADLs) to model scalable architectures, ensuring DTs can grow with system complexity.

5. **Deployment Models Supported**: The document implies support for **containerized deployments** (e.g., Docker, Kubernetes) and **microservices architectures** to modularize DT components. Hybrid deployment (e.g., cloud-edge-on-premise) is also suggested to balance cost, latency, and reliability.

6. **Availability/Reliability Assurance**: Reliability is ensured through **redundant hardware**, **automated failover**, and **disaster recovery plans**. The document does not detail specific SLAs (e.g., 99.9% uptime) but aligns with industrial practices for **high-availability DT systems**.
"
]

instance horizontal_integration : DTDFVocab:HoriIntegration [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin system’s horizontal integration with external systems is implicitly addressed through the **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** framework, which requires integration of **heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives** across multiple modeling notations (e.g., **SysML**, **ADLs**) and tools (e.g., **Simulink**, **C++/Java**). While no explicit **data exchange protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, REST) or **interoperability standards** (e.g., ISO/IEC 42010, IEEE 1471) are mentioned, the need to aggregate and abstract data for Digital Shadows suggests reliance on **standardized data formats** (e.g., JSON, XML) or **enterprise integration patterns** (e.g., ESBs, APIs). The **Digital Shadow Reference Model** does not detail dependencies on external systems, but the MBSE context implies that Digital Twin systems may interact with **enterprise systems** (e.g., ERP, MES) or **other Digital Twins** via **interoperable interfaces** to support cross-system collaboration. No mention of **integration patterns** (e.g., microservices, SOA) or **external system dependencies** is provided."
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system adheres to **industry standards** for **modeling languages** (e.g., **SysML v1.7** for functional/logical architecture, **AADL** for avionics systems, **ACME** for architecture description) and **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink** for dynamic behavior). **Interoperability standards** are implied through **heterogeneous tool integration** (per [31]), where models are exchanged across **SysML, ADLs, and general-purpose languages (C++, Java)**. **Compliance frameworks** are not explicitly mentioned, but **methodology standardization** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) ensures consistency in **abstraction layers** and **transition management**. **Certification requirements** are not detailed, though **validation via simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink) aligns with **ISO/IEC 42010** (system modeling) and **IEEE 1517** (systems engineering processes) principles. **Interoperability** is achieved via **shared modeling notations** (e.g., SysML IBDs for component-connector perspectives) and **toolchain compatibility**, though explicit protocols (e.g., OPC UA, ROS) are not referenced."
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The Digital Twin system for the MABI MAX 100 serial robot incorporates multiple **safety-critical mechanisms** and **cybersecurity protocols** to ensure operational integrity and secure data handling. The **safety cell** restricts the maximum working range of the first axis to [-90°, 90°] and enforces a **maximum speed limit of |q̇_max| = 75°/s**, mitigating physical risks such as collisions or excessive motion. The system employs **dual-position encoders** (motor encoder and secondary encoder behind the gearbox) to reduce **backlash and positional uncertainty**, enabling real-time monitoring of drive train elasticity and friction—critical for fail-safe operations.

For **cybersecurity**, the robot is controlled via the **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl. CNC controller**, which supports **access control mechanisms** through role-based permissions for position, speed, and acceleration inputs/outputs. The controller enforces **authentication systems** for command execution, ensuring only authorized processes (e.g., subtractive manufacturing tasks) modify motor current (I_M) or torque (τ_M). **Threat mitigation** is implicitly addressed via the **closed-loop control** of the cycloidal gear transmission, where torque outputs (τ_G, τ_out) are validated against motor constants to prevent unauthorized torque overrides.

**Risk assessment** is embedded in the system’s **safety cell constraints**, which act as a physical barrier to unauthorized motion outside predefined operational envelopes. The **motor current-to-torque conversion** (using known motor constants) ensures **safety-critical torque monitoring**, while the **dual-encoder arrangement** provides redundancy for positional accuracy (±0.06 mm repeatability, ±0.1 mm positional accuracy). No explicit mention of **data protection regulations (e.g., GDPR), anonymization, or sharing agreements** is provided in the documents."
]
